title: Learning a few words of Tagalog from my neighbour
summary: A small story about how a few language scraps built a real friendship.
topic: Learning a few words of Tagalog from my neighbour
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences (150–250 words) about a person who taught you a few words in their language. Describe how it changed your friendship. Use past simple, past continuous, and 'while'.
hintStarters: When I moved to Manila, my neighbour was a small woman named…
Every morning while she was watering her plants, she taught me…
I only learned a few words, but…
sampleText: When I moved to a small flat in Manila, my neighbour on the right was a kind woman named Tita Lourdes. She was sixty-two and had retired from a long career as a primary-school teacher. On my second morning, she knocked on the door and gave me a paper plate with two warm pieces of pandesal. While she was watering her plants on the shared balcony, she asked me where I was from and how long I planned to stay. My Filipino was nothing. I only knew the word 'salamat' from a song. She laughed kindly and said, 'I will teach you one word every morning.' For two months, I really did learn one word a day. Some were very simple, like 'tubig' (water) and 'gutom' (hungry). Some were short phrases I needed for the market, like 'magkano ito' (how much is this). She corrected my pronunciation slowly, without ever making me feel stupid. We never had long conversations, but each small word felt like a small gift. When I moved out a year later, she gave me a notebook with all the words we had practised. The notebook was almost full. I learned that you do not need a whole language to build a real friendship with someone.

Bà sửa cách phát âm cho tôi từ tốn

sampleAnnotations.phrase: without ever making me feel stupid
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: mà không bao giờ làm tôi cảm thấy ngu ngốc

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past continuous with 'while'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'While + past continuous' đặt bối cảnh đang diễn ra: 'While she was watering her plants on the shared balcony, she asked me where I was from.' Past simple cho hành động chen vào.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'While + past continuous' sets the ongoing background: 'While she was watering her plants on the shared balcony, she asked me where I was from.' Past simple is used for the interrupting event.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Emphasis with 'did' in the past
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Did + V (base form)' trong câu khẳng định nhấn mạnh ý: 'I really did learn one word a day.' = Tôi thực sự đã học một từ mỗi ngày.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Did + base verb' in an affirmative sentence adds emphasis: 'I really did learn one word a day.' = I really did, not just claimed to.

sampleTranslationVi: Khi tôi chuyển đến một căn hộ nhỏ ở Manila, người hàng xóm bên phải tôi là một người phụ nữ tốt bụng tên là Tita Lourdes. Bà sáu mươi hai tuổi và đã nghỉ hưu sau một sự nghiệp dài làm giáo viên tiểu học. Vào buổi sáng thứ hai của tôi, bà gõ cửa và đưa cho tôi một đĩa giấy có hai miếng bánh mì pandesal ấm. Trong khi bà đang tưới cây trên ban công chung, bà hỏi tôi đến từ đâu và dự định ở lại bao lâu. Tiếng Filipino của tôi không có gì cả. Tôi chỉ biết từ 'salamat' từ một bài hát. Bà cười dịu dàng và nói, 'Tôi sẽ dạy bạn một từ mỗi sáng.' Trong hai tháng, tôi thực sự đã học được một từ mỗi ngày. Một số rất đơn giản, như 'tubig' (nước) và 'gutom' (đói). Một số là cụm từ ngắn mà tôi cần ở chợ, như 'magkano ito' (cái này bao nhiêu). Bà sửa cách phát âm cho tôi từ tốn, mà không bao giờ làm tôi cảm thấy ngu ngốc. Chúng tôi không bao giờ có những cuộc trò chuyện dài, nhưng mỗi từ nhỏ đều giống như một món quà nhỏ. Khi tôi dọn đi một năm sau đó, bà tặng tôi một cuốn sổ có tất cả những từ chúng tôi đã thực hành. Cuốn sổ gần như đã đầy. Tôi học được rằng bạn không cần cả một ngôn ngữ để xây dựng một tình bạn thực sự với ai đó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct 'while' + past continuous sentence.
options: While she watered her plants, she asked me where I was from.
While she is watering her plants, she asked me where I was from.
While she was water her plants, she asked me where I was from.
While she was watering her plants, she asked me where I was from.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'While + past continuous' for the background; past simple for the question.

options: She asked me where I was from.
She asked me where was I from.
She asked me where am I from.
She asked me from where I was.
mcQuestions.explanation: Embedded questions keep statement word order.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct emphatic past form.
options: I really do learned one word a day.
I really did learn one word a day.
I really did learned one word a day.
I really am learn one word a day.
rich and famous
very strict in teaching
calm and able to wait without anger
young and energetic
true my pronunciation
right my pronunciation
fix my pronunciation good
correct my pronunciation
all the words we have practised
all the words we practising
all the words we have practise
all the words we did practise
to give someone a gift
to say hello to someone when you meet them
to make someone food
to write a letter to someone

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct word order with 'without ever'.
options: without making me ever feel stupid
without making ever me feel stupid
without ever making me feel stupid
ever without making me feel stupid
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Without ever + V-ing' is the natural pattern.

options: felt as a small gift
felt for a small gift
felt to be a small gift
felt like a small gift
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Feel like + noun' is the natural comparative collocation.

options: a kind of Filipino bread roll
a kind of Filipino dance
a kind of Filipino fish
a kind of Filipino fruit
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Pandesal' is a soft Filipino bread roll, often eaten for breakfast.

options: You do not need a whole language for build a real friendship.
You do not need a whole language to build a real friendship.
You do not need a whole language to building a real friendship.
You do not need a whole language for to build a real friendship.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Why can a few well-chosen words in another person's language sometimes do more for a friendship than fluent conversation in a shared language?
